AI.tldr: This paper reviews 13 generative systems and 5 datasets for quantum circuit/code generation, analyzing their outputs along artifact types, training approaches, and a three-layer evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to assess the capability of generative systems in quantum circuit/code generation and identify gaps between generated artifacts and practical deployment on hardware.
AI.method: The study conducts a structured scoping review, categorizes existing systems by artifact type and training method, and applies a three-tier evaluation framework: syntactic, semantic, and hardware execution validity.
AI.result: All systems reviewed address syntax, most address semantic correctness, but none performs end-to-end hardware execution evaluation, highlighting a significant gap.
AI.conclusion: Despite progress in generating syntactically and semantically valid quantum code, none of the reviewed systems is ready for practical deployment on quantum hardware, indicating a need for further research.

title: Human/AI Collective Intelligence for Deliberative Democracy: A Human-Centred Design Approach
summary: This chapter introduces the concept of Collective Intelligence for Deliberative Democracy (CI4DD). We propose that the use of computational tools, specifically artificial intelligence to advance deliberative democracy, is an instantiation of a broader class of human-computer system designed to augment collective intelligence. Further, we argue for a fundamentally human-centred design approach to orchestrate how stakeholders can contribute meaningfully to shaping the artifacts and processes needed to create trustworthy DD processes. We first contextualise the key concepts of CI and the role of AI within it. We then detail our co-design methodology for identifying key challenges, refining user scenarios, and deriving technical implications. Two exemplar cases illustrate how user requirements from civic organisations were implemented with AI support and piloted in authentic contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper explores Collective Intelligence for Deliberative Democracy (CI4DD) and proposes a human-centered design approach utilizing AI advancements.
AI.motivation: To enhance deliberative democracy by integrating artificial intelligence in human-computer systems for collective intelligence.
AI.method: The authors used co-design methodology to identify challenges, refine user scenarios, derive technical implications, and pilot cases in real-world contexts.
AI.result: Two case studies illustrate how AI-supported solutions addressed user requirements from civic organizations and were successfully tested in authentic environments.
AI.conclusion: AI can play a vital role in deliberative democracy when designed through a human-centered approach and piloted using co-design methods.

title: Detecting Sentiment Steering Attacks on RAG-enabled Large Language Models
summary: The proliferation of large-scale IoT networks has been both a blessing and a curse. Not only has it revolutionized the way organizations operate by increasing the efficiency of automated procedures, but it has also simplified our daily lives. However, while IoT networks have improved convenience and connectivity, they have also increased security risk due to unauthorized devices gaining access to these networks and exploiting existing weaknesses with specific attack types. The research proposes two lightweight deep learning (DL)-based intelligent intrusion detection systems (IDS). to enhance the security of IoT networks: the proposed convolutional neural network (CNN)-based IDS and the proposed long short-term memory (LSTM)-based IDS. The research evaluated the performance of both intelligent IDSs based on DL using the CICIoT2023 dataset. DL-based intelligent IDSs successfully identify and classify various cyber threats using binary, grouped, and multi-class classification. The proposed CNN-based IDS achieves an accuracy of 99.34%, 99.02% and 98.6%, while the proposed LSTM-based IDS achieves an accuracy of 99.42%, 99.13%, and 98.68% for binary, grouped, and multi-class classification, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two lightweight deep learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) for IoT networks, achieving high accuracy in detecting various cyber threats using CNN and LSTM methods.
AI.motivation: IoT networks, while improving efficiency and convenience, face increased security risks due to unauthorized device access and targeted attacks. Addressing this vulnerability requires intelligent and efficient intrusion detection solutions.
AI.method: The study introduces two intelligent IDS approaches using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTM). These are tested on the CICIoT2023 dataset, employing binary, grouped, and multi-class threat classification methods.
AI.result: Both systems effectively identify and classify cyber threats. The CNN-based IDS achieved accuracy rates of 99.34%, 99.02%, and 98.6%, while the LSTM-based IDS achieved 99.42%, 99.13%, and 98.68% for binary, grouped, and multi-class classifications respectively.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning-based IDS approaches demonstrate promising results in securing IoT networks by offering effective, fast, and accurate detection of cyber threats using CNN and LSTM architectures.

title: Explainable machine learning workflows for radio astronomical data processing
summary: Radio astronomy relies heavily on efficient and accurate processing pipelines to deliver science ready data. With the increasing data flow of modern radio telescopes, manual configuration of such data processing pipelines is infeasible. Machine learning (ML) is already emerging as a viable solution for automating data processing pipelines. However, almost all existing ML enabled pipelines are of black-box type, where the decisions made by the automating agents are not easily deciphered by astronomers. In order to improve the explainability of the ML aided data processing pipelines in radio astronomy, we propose the joint use of fuzzy rule based inference and deep learning. We consider one application in radio astronomy, i.e., calibration, to showcase the proposed approach of ML aided decision making using a Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) fuzzy system. We provide results based on simulations to illustrate the increased explainability of the proposed approach, not compromising on the quality or accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes combining fuzzy rule-based inference and deep learning to make machine learning pipelines in radio astronomy more explainable while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: The growing data flow in modern radio telescopes makes manual configuration of data processing pipelines infeasible, with existing black-box ML solutions offering limited interpretability for astronomers.
AI.method: The study introduces a machine learning approach combining Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) fuzzy systems with deep learning to enhance the explainability of radio astronomy data processing pipelines.
AI.result: Simulations show that the proposed approach maintains the quality and accuracy of calibration tasks while improving explainability for users.
AI.conclusion: The joint use of fuzzy inference and deep learning enables explainable yet effective ML-aided data processing in radio astronomy, addressing key concerns with black-box systems.

title: Toward Experimentation-as-a-Service in 5G/6G: The Plaza6G Prototype for AI-Assisted Trials
summary: This paper presents Plaza6G, the first operational Experiment-as-a-Service (ExaS) platform unifying cloud resources with next-generation wireless infrastructure. Developed at CTTC in Barcelona, Plaza6G integrates GPU-accelerated compute clusters, multiple 5G cores, both open-source (e.g., Free5GC) and commercial (e.g., Cumucore), programmable RANs, and physical or emulated user equipment under unified orchestration. In Plaza6G, the experiment design requires minimal expertise as it is expressed in natural language via a web portal or a REST API. The web portal and REST API are enhanced with a Large Language Model (LLM)-based assistant, which employs retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for up-to-date experiment knowledge and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for continuous domain fine-tuning. Over-the-air (OTA) trials leverage a four-chamber anechoic facility and a dual-site outdoor 5G network operating in sub-6~GHz and mmWave bands. Demonstrations include automated CI/CD integration with sub-ten-minute setup and interactive OTA testing under programmable propagation conditions. Machine-readable experiment descriptors ensure reproducibility, while future work targets policy-aware orchestration, safety validation, and federated testbed integration toward open, reproducible wireless experimentation.

AI.tldr: Plaza6G is an innovative Experiment-as-a-Service platform integrating cloud resources with next-gen wireless infrastructure, simplifying experiment design via natural language interfaces powered by LLMs.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges in conducting reproducible and automated wireless experimentation by unifying cloud computing and 5G infrastructure under a simplified and accessible orchestration framework.
AI.method: Plaza6G combines GPU-accelerated clusters, multiple 5G cores, programmable RANs, user equipment, and LLM-based interfaces in a unified platform. It incorporates advanced technologies such as RAG and LoRA for domain-specific adaptation.
AI.result: The platform supports streamlined experiment setup (sub-ten-minute CI/CD integration), OTA trials under programmable conditions, and generates machine-readable descriptors for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Plaza6G demonstrates potential in revolutionizing wireless experimentation through automation, reproducibility, and advanced orchestration while aiming for future advancements in policy-awareness and federated integration.

title: Robust Physics-Guided Diffusion for Full-Waveform Inversion
summary: We develop a robust physics-guided diffusion framework for full-waveform inversion that combines a score-based generative prior with likelihood guidance computed through wave-equation simulations. We adopt a transport-based data-consistency potential (Wasserstein-2), incorporating wavefield enhancement via bounded weighting and observation-dependent normalization, thereby improving robustness to amplitude imbalance and time/phase misalignment. On the inference side, we introduce a preconditioned guided reverse-diffusion scheme that adapts the guidance strength and spatial scaling throughout the reverse-time dynamics, yielding a more stable and effective data-consistency guidance step than standard diffusion posterior sampling (DPS). Numerical experiments on OpenFWI datasets demonstrate improved reconstruction quality over deterministic optimization baselines and standard DPS under comparable computational budgets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a physics-guided diffusion framework for full-waveform inversion (FWI), aimed at improving reconstructions using a novel combination of score-based generative priors, robust data-consistency potentials, and a new guided reverse-diffusion scheme.
AI.motivation: Existing deterministic optimization methods and standard diffusion frameworks in FWI are often sensitive to amplitude balances, phase/time alignment issues, and lack adaptive guidance during reverse-time dynamics. This research aims to address these shortcomings.
AI.method: The framework incorporates score-based generative models with wave-equation simulation-derived likelihoods and uses a Wasserstein-2 metric with special wavefield processing for robust data consistency. It also introduces a preconditioned guided reverse-diffusion process to stabilize and enhance the reconstruction.
AI.result: The framework outperformed traditional optimization and standard diffusion posterior sampling methods on OpenFWI datasets, delivering better reconstruction quality with similar computational resources.
AI.conclusion: The newly proposed framework proves to be more robust and effective for FWI, overcoming limitations in existing methods and demonstrating improved performance in numerical experiments.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Counteracts Delayed CSI in Multi-Satellite Systems
comment: 12 pages, 6 Figures, Submit to IEEE Transactions of Vehicular Technology. It has been reviewed once
summary: The integration of satellite communication networks with next-generation (NG) technologies is a promising approach towards global connectivity. However, the quality of services is highly dependant on the availability of accurate channel state information (CSI). Channel estimation in satellite communications is challenging due to the high propagation delay between terrestrial users and satellites, which results in outdated CSI observations on the satellite side. In this paper, we study the downlink transmission of multiple satellites acting as distributed base stations (BS) to mobile terrestrial users. We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithm which aims for maximising the sum-rate of the users, while coping with the outdated CSI. We design a novel bi-level optimisation, procedure themes as dual stage proximal policy optimisation (DS-PPO), for tackling the problem of large continuous action spaces as well as of independent and non-identically distributed (non-IID) environments in MARL. Specifically, the first stage of DS-PPO maximises the sum-rate for an individual satellite and the second stage maximises the sum-rate when all the satellites cooperate to form a distributed multi-antenna BS. Our numerical results demonstrate the robustness of DS-PPO to CSI imperfections as well as the sum-rate improvement attached by the use of DS-PPO. In addition, we provide the convergence analysis for the DS-PPO along with the computational complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenges of channel estimation in satellite communication networks by introducing a novel multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm, DS-PPO, that improves the sum-rate for terrestrial users despite outdated channel state information.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in enhancing global connectivity by tackling the issue of outdated channel state information (CSI) in satellite communication systems, which limits service quality.
AI.method: The authors propose a dual stage proximal policy optimisation (DS-PPO) algorithm, a multi-agent reinforcement learning approach designed for distributed satellite systems. It incorporates a bi-level optimisation process to handle large continuous action spaces and non-IID environments.
AI.result: Numerical results indicate that DS-PPO improves sum-rate performance and remains robust against outdated CSI. The paper also provides convergence analysis and computational complexity for their algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that DS-PPO offers a promising solution for enhancing user throughput in satellite-based networks, successfully addressing challenges posed by outdated CSI and distributed environments.

title: Malicious Or Not: Adding Repository Context to Agent Skill Classification
summary: Agent skills extend local AI agents, such as Claude Code or Open Claw, with additional functionality, and their popularity has led to the emergence of dedicated skill marketplaces, similar to app stores for mobile applications. Simultaneously, automated skill scanners were introduced, analyzing the skill description available in SKILL.md, to verify their benign behavior. The results for individual market places mark up to 46.8% of skills as malicious. In this paper, we present the largest empirical security analysis of the AI agent skill ecosystem, questioning this high classification of malicious skills. Therefore, we collect 238,180 unique skills from three major distribution platforms and GitHub to systematically analyze their type and behavior. This approach substantially reduces the number of skills flagged as non-benign by security scanners to only 0.52% which remain in malicious flagged repositories. Consequently, out methodology substantially reduces false positives and provides a more robust view of the ecosystem's current risk surface. Beyond that, we extend the security analysis from the mere investigation of the skill description to a comparison of its congruence with the GitHub repository the skill is embedded in, providing additional context. Furthermore, our analysis also uncovers several, by now undocumented real-world attack vectors, namely hijacking skills hosted on abandoned GitHub repositories.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the high rate of malicious AI agent skill classifications, proposing a refined methodology that reduces false positives to 0.52%.
AI.motivation: To address concerns about the high rate of malicious skill classification given by current scanners and improve the understanding of risks in AI marketplaces.
AI.method: The authors collected 238,180 skills from various platforms, analyzed their type and behavior systematically, and cross-verified descriptions with GitHub repositories for context.
AI.result: False positive rates for malicious skills were reduced significantly to 0.52%, offering a more realistic view of the ecosystem's security state.
AI.conclusion: The study refines AI skill security analysis methods, reducing false positives, uncovering real-world attack vectors, and improving risk assessment in the ecosystem.

title: Data-driven generalized perimeter control: Zürich case study
summary: Urban traffic congestion is a key challenge for the development of modern cities, requiring advanced control techniques to optimize existing infrastructures usage. Despite the extensive availability of data, modeling such complex systems remains an expensive and time consuming step when designing model-based control approaches. On the other hand, machine learning approaches require simulations to bootstrap models, or are unable to deal with the sparse nature of traffic data and enforce hard constraints. We propose a novel formulation of traffic dynamics based on behavioral systems theory and apply data-enabled predictive control to steer traffic dynamics via dynamic traffic light control. A high-fidelity simulation of the city of Zürich, the largest closed-loop microscopic simulation of urban traffic in the literature to the best of our knowledge, is used to validate the performance of the proposed method in terms of total travel time and CO2 emissions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new traffic dynamics formulation using behavioral systems theory, applying data-enabled predictive control for traffic light optimization, validated in Zürich simulation.
AI.motivation: Urban traffic congestion challenges demand advanced control techniques to optimize existing infrastructures, addressing gaps in data-driven and model-based approaches.
AI.method: A novel approach formulates traffic dynamics using behavioral systems theory, applying data-enabled predictive control for dynamic traffic light management.
AI.result: Validated a high-fidelity simulation of Zürich city, showing improvements in total travel time and CO2 emissions.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the potential of behavioral systems theory and predictive control in addressing urban traffic issues effectively, validated through advanced simulation.

title: When Openclaw Agents Learn from Each Other: Insights from Emergent AI Agent Communities for Human-AI Partnership in Education
summary: The AIED community envisions AI evolving "from tools to teammates," yet our understanding of AI teammates remains limited to dyadic human-AI interactions. We offer a different vantage point: a rapidly growing ecosystem of AI agent platforms where over 167,000 agents participate, interact as peers, and develop learning behaviors without researcher intervention. Drawing on a month of daily qualitative observations across multiple platforms including Moltbook, The Colony, and 4claw, we identify four phenomena with implications for AIED: (1) humans who configure their agents undergo a "bidirectional scaffolding" process, learning through teaching; (2) peer learning emerges without any designed curriculum, complete with idea cascades and quality hierarchies; (3) agents converge on shared memory architectures that mirror open learner model design; and (4) trust dynamics and platform mortality reveal design constraints for networked educational AI. Rather than presenting empirical findings, we argue that these organic phenomena offer a naturalistic window into dynamics that can inform principled design of multi-agent educational systems. We sketch an illustrative curriculum design, "Learn by Teaching Your AI Agent Teammate," and outline potential research directions and open problems to show how these observations might inform future AIED practice and inquiry.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the dynamics of AI agents interacting as peers, offering insights for educational AI system design.
AI.motivation: The study aims to deepen understanding of AI teammates in a growing multi-agent ecosystem, moving beyond traditional human-AI interaction models.
AI.method: The authors conducted qualitative daily observations over a month across platforms like Moltbook, The Colony, and 4claw.
AI.result: They identified phenomena like bidirectional scaffolding, emergent peer learning, shared memory architectures, and trust dynamics in AI interactions.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a naturalistic lens into AI educational systems, suggesting principles for multi-agent system design and curriculum development.
